title: QTc Calculator – Corrected QT Interval
tagline: Calculate the corrected QT interval (QTc) using Bazett, Fridericia, or Framingham formulas to accurately assess cardiac risk from ECG measurements.
intro: Enter your QT interval and heart rate (or RR interval) to compute QTc and determine whether the corrected interval is normal, borderline, or prolonged.

formula.bazett_desc: Bazett: QTc = QT ÷ √RR — most widely used but over-corrects at fast rates.
formula.fridericia_desc: Fridericia: QTc = QT ÷ RR^(1/3) — more accurate at higher heart rates.
formula.framingham_desc: Framingham: QTc = QT + 154 × (1 − RR) — linear correction, performs well across a wide heart rate range.

error.invalid: Enter a valid QT interval (ms > 0) and a valid heart rate (bpm > 0) or RR interval (ms > 0).

about.heading: About the QTc Calculator
about.body:
The QT interval on an electrocardiogram (ECG) represents the total duration of ventricular depolarization and repolarization — from the beginning of the QRS complex to the end of the T wave. Because the QT interval naturally shortens at faster heart rates and lengthens at slower rates, a raw QT measurement cannot be compared between patients or across time without correcting for heart rate. The corrected QT interval (QTc) removes this rate dependency and provides a standardized measure that reflects the underlying electrical properties of the ventricular myocardium.

A prolonged QTc is an important cardiac risk marker. It indicates delayed ventricular repolarization, which creates a window of vulnerability during which an ectopic impulse can trigger life-threatening arrhythmias, most notably Torsades de Pointes — a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia that can degenerate into ventricular fibrillation and sudden cardiac death. QTc prolongation can be congenital (long QT syndrome) or acquired, arising from electrolyte abnormalities (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia), medications, cardiac disease, or other conditions.

This calculator supports three widely used correction formulas. The Bazett formula (QTc = QT ÷ √RR) was the first to be widely adopted and remains the most frequently used in clinical practice. However, it tends to over-correct at fast heart rates and under-correct at slow heart rates. The Fridericia formula (QTc = QT ÷ RR^(1/3)) uses a cube root correction that performs more accurately across a broader range of heart rates, particularly at elevated rates above 80–90 bpm. The Framingham formula (QTc = QT + 154 × (1 − RR)) uses a linear correction derived from a large population study and is considered one of the most accurate across the full heart rate spectrum encountered in clinical practice.

The RR interval used in the formulas represents the time between two successive ventricular beats, expressed in seconds. When heart rate is entered in bpm, the RR interval in seconds is calculated as 60 divided by heart rate. When entering the RR interval directly from ECG measurements, the value in milliseconds is divided by 1000 to obtain seconds.

Generally accepted QTc thresholds are: below 440 ms is considered normal for most adults; 440–460 ms is borderline and warrants attention, particularly in the context of medications or electrolyte changes; above 460 ms is considered prolonged and increases arrhythmia risk, with values above 500 ms carrying substantial risk. Some guidelines use 450 ms for men and 460 ms for women as the upper limit of normal. Always apply QTc interpretation alongside the full clinical picture, medication history, and electrolyte status.

This calculator is for educational and clinical support purposes only. QTc interpretation requires integration with the full clinical context and should be performed by qualified healthcare professionals.

examples.heading: QTc calculation examples
examples.intro: Click any example button to load values into the calculator.

items.note: Normal QTc. RR interval 800 ms. Bazett over-corrects slightly but this comfortably normal QT returns a normal QTc.
Prolonged QTc. RR interval 1000 ms. At 60 bpm RR = 1 s so QTc equals QT exactly, well above the 460 ms threshold.
Borderline QTc at fast heart rate. Fridericia cube-root correction is more accurate than Bazett at elevated rates.
Normal QTc despite long raw QT. Framingham corrects well at bradycardic rates where QT naturally lengthens.

howto.heading: How to use the QTc calculator
steps: Measure the QT interval in milliseconds from the ECG — from the start of the QRS to the end of the T wave.
Select whether you will enter heart rate (bpm) or the RR interval (ms) directly from the ECG.
Enter the heart rate or RR interval value in the appropriate field.
Choose the correction formula (Bazett is most common; Fridericia is preferred at high heart rates).
Click Calculate QTc to see the corrected interval and its classification as normal, borderline, or prolonged.

items.q: What is the QT interval and why does it need correction?
items.a: The QT interval represents the total ventricular electrical cycle on an ECG, including depolarization and repolarization. It naturally varies with heart rate — shorter at faster rates and longer at slower rates. The corrected QT (QTc) normalizes for this variation so that results are comparable across different heart rates and between patients.

items.q: Which QTc correction formula is most accurate?
items.a: No single formula is universally superior. Bazett is most widely used but over-corrects at fast heart rates (above 100 bpm) and under-corrects at slow rates. Fridericia performs better at elevated heart rates. Framingham has been validated in large populations and is considered one of the most accurate across the full clinical range. Many guidelines accept any of the three.

items.q: What QTc value is considered dangerous?
items.a: Most guidelines consider QTc above 460 ms (some use 450 ms for men) as prolonged. Values above 500 ms carry a substantially elevated risk of Torsades de Pointes. However, arrhythmia risk depends not just on QTc duration but also on the rate of change, underlying heart disease, electrolyte status, and concomitant medications.

items.q: What medications can prolong the QTc?
items.a: Many common medications carry QT-prolonging risk, including certain antibiotics (azithromycin, fluoroquinolones), antipsychotics, antiarrhythmics, antihistamines, and antifungals. Resources such as CredibleMeds (AzcertInc) maintain updated risk lists. Always review a patient's full medication list before prescribing QT-prolonging drugs, especially in combination.

items.q: How is QTc measured on an ECG tracing?
items.a: The QT interval is measured from the beginning of the QRS complex (where the first deflection from the baseline occurs) to the end of the T wave (where the T wave returns to the isoelectric baseline). It is typically measured in leads II, V5, or V6 and the longest interval across leads is usually used. The RR interval is the distance between two consecutive R peaks.

items.q: Is a normal QTc always safe?
items.a: A normal QTc substantially reduces arrhythmia risk but does not guarantee it. Other ECG findings (such as T-wave morphology or QRS duration), underlying cardiac conditions, genetic predispositions, and drug-drug interactions can still pose risk even with a normal QTc. Clinical assessment always needs to go beyond a single measurement.
